MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-05-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Attys. WALT CUBBERLY, Esq. of Williams Hart Boundas Easterby, LLP (Tel: 713-230-2200) and JESSICA DAYTON, Esq. of ADZ Law, LLP (Tel: 650-458-2300) for plaintiff;
Attys. GARY A. WOLENSKY, Esq. and ROBERT ATKINS, Esq. of Buchalter (Tel: 949-760-1121) for Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc. and Rasier, LLC.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. AND RAISIER, LLC'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc. and Rasier, LLC's motion to strike is denied in part and granted in part. Plaintiff's "allegations for the purpose of prejudicing Uber" are colorful, but within the realm of acceptable pleading, particularly for background. Because the basis for punitive damages is pled with insufficient particularity, that part of the motion is granted with leave to amend.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-05-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Attys. WALT CUBBERLY, Esq. of Williams Hart Boundas Easterby, LLP (Tel: 713-230-2200) and JESSICA DAYTON, Esq. of ADZ Law, LLP (Tel: 650-458-2300) for plaintiff;
Attys. GARY A. WOLENSKY, Esq. and ROBERT ATKINS, Esq. of Buchalter (Tel: 949-760-1121) for Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc. and Rasier, LLC.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. AND RASIER, LLC'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc. and Rasier, LLC's demurrers to six of the complaint's numbered causes of action are sustained or overruled as follows:
4-7 (false imprisonment, assault, sexual battery, intentional infliction of emotional distress). Defendants' demurrer ignores the complaint's allegations that Uber is a common carrier and thus liable for the tortious conduct of its drivers. (Cmpl., par. 56.) These allegations may or may not prove factually founded, but that is not for resolution at the pleading state. This demurrer is overruled.
8 (fraud). The court sustains the demurrer to the fraud cause of action without leave to amend based on claim preclusion. Plaintiff's claim is predicated on Uber's alleged statements regarding "safe rides" and background checks. Those claims were previously asserted and resolved as part of a class action settlement in McKnight v. Uber Technologies, Inc., 4:14-cv-05615-JST (N.D. Cal. Jun. 1, 2017), ECF No. 125. An amended (and final) Stipulation of Settlement (the "Settlement") was reached and entered on June 1, 2017. Id. 4:14-cv-05615-JST (N.D. Cal. Jun. 1, 2017), ECF No. 125. Plaintiff does not address this issue in her opposition and apparently concedes that she was a member of the Class defined in the Settlement and her fraud claim is therefore precluded by the Settlement and the Release. (See Louie v. BFS Retail & Commercial Operations LLC (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 1544, 1555 ["[A] judgment in a class action is binding on class members in any subsequent litigation"]; Villacres v. ABM Indus. Inc. (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 562, 577 ["Res judicata applies to a court-approved settlement agreement in a class action dismissed with prejudice."].)
9 (Ralph Act). The complaint adequately pled the elements of a Ralph Act claim as to driver Doe One. (Cmplt., 21:15-22:11; CACI 3063.) Plaintiff's opposition suggests she can also plead theories of Ralph Act liability against the demurring defendants, but has yet to do so. This demurrer is sustained with leave to amend.

Order signed.
